race.name: Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo
race.tagline: Ride the legendary Flemish classic's course through war history and cobbled climbs

climate.description: Late March in West Flanders brings cool, variable spring conditions typical of the Flemish coast. Expect wind exposure across polders and coastal sections, with potential for rain and echelon-forming crosswinds.
challenges: Precipitation: High (typical for Belgian spring classics season)

features: Mixed terrain combining coastal polders, cobbled Flemish hills, and French rural roads
The signature Kemmelberg features steep cobbled sections (20% gradient)
Routes include multiple ascents of iconic climbs and sections of unpaved Plugstreets

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 71. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: A prestige-laden Flemish spring classic featuring wind-exposed polders, cobbled climbs, and war-history landmarks. Less mountainous than Ardennes classics but technically demanding with steep, unpredictable terrain. Routes emphasize the Kemmelberg's notorious 20% cobbled gradient and multiple ascents of Baneberg, Monteberg, and Plugstreets' unpaved sections. Finish is flat after final Kemmelberg descent, allowing for sprint finishes. Strong headwind exposure and echelon-forming conditions typical.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Kemmelberg: A steep (20% gradient), cobbled climb ascended twice in the race finale. The descent is notoriously technical and dangerous, with unpredictable terrain and tight turns. Riders must manage both the physical demand and technical bike-handling skill; crashes are common in the professional race.
suffering_zones: Kemmelberg: Twice-ascended cobbled climb (20% gradient, technical descent); most iconic and hardest section of the race
Plugstreets: Unpaved Franco-Belgian roads with multiple ascents; technical and energy-sapping
Coastal polders (Middelkerke to Veurne): Long, exposed, wind-swept sections prone to echelon formation
Heuvelland hill zone: Succession of Baneberg, Monteberg, Kemmelberg within 12 km; narrow technical descents
Franco-Flemish hills (Kasselberg, Scherpenberg, Katsberg, Berthen): Steep, short climbs with minimal recovery

logistics.airport: BRU (Brussels Airport (Zaventem)), 100km; OST (Ostend-Bruges Airport), 50km; LIL (Lille Airport (France)), 40km
logistics.lodging_strategy: Wevelgem and surrounding towns (Ypres, Heuvelland) offer hotels, B&Bs, and cycling-focused lodging. Book early for late March; many riders combine with E3 Saxo Bank Classic weekend.
logistics.transport: Wevelgem-based event with straightforward logistics in West Flanders. Start and finish in Wevelgem town center (Vanackerestraat); some 2026 routes incorporate Middelkerke coastal start aligning with professional race changes. Well-established infrastructure with team paddock, feed stations, and post-race facilities.

history.origin_story: Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo is the amateur/sportive version of the professional Gent-Wevelgem classic, held annually the day before the pro race (Saturday in late March). The professional race has run since 1934, created as a tribute to cyclist Gaston Rebry from Wevelgem. In 2026, both pro and cyclo events rebrand as 'In Flanders Fields - From Middelkerke to Wevelgem,' incorporating the new pro race start location on the Belgian coast while maintaining the traditional Wevelgem finish.
notable_moments: Professional race established 1934 in honor of Wevelgem cyclist Gaston Rebry
2008: Pro race upgraded to UCI Pro Tour status; course distance increased to 235 km with added Heuvelland climbs
2010: Franco-Flemish hills (Kasselberg, Scherpenberg, Katsberg, Berthen) reintroduced to course
2010s: Ypres and Menin Gate incorporated into race finale, linking cycling to WWI 'In Flanders Fields' history
2026: Race rebrands to 'In Flanders Fields - From Middelkerke to Wevelgem'; start moves to Middelkerke coast; cyclo event follows same route changes

biased_opinion.summary: Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo is the thinking cyclist's spring classic—a chance to suffer on the same cobbles and climbs that define professional racing's most storied one-day event. The Kemmelberg will humble you, the Plugstreets will test your bike handling, and passing through Ypres's Menin Gate adds gravitas that most sportives lack. Late March weather is unpredictable and often brutal, but that's part of the Flemish classic experience. If you want to understand why cyclists revere the spring classics, this is essential. Not the easiest or most scenic ride, but undeniably authentic.

final_verdict.one_liner: A prestige-laden, historically rich mirror of a UCI WorldTour classic with multiple distance options and genuine Flemish character—ideal for riders seeking authentic spring classic experience.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo delivers what few sportives can: direct access to a legendary professional race's course, complete with the Kemmelberg's infamous cobbles, Plugstreets' technical unpaved sections, and the emotional weight of riding through Ypres's Menin Gate. The 2026 edition's alignment with the pro race's new Middelkerke-to-Wevelgem route ensures you're riding current UCI WorldTour terrain. Multiple distance options (65–220 km) make it accessible to various fitness levels, though even the shortest routes include serious climbing and technical descents. Late March conditions are typically cool, wet, and windy—classic Flemish spring racing weather that adds authenticity but demands preparation. At an estimated €47–60 entry with 5,000 participants (40% international), it offers strong community and prestige without premium pricing. Best suited for riders who value cycling history, don't mind suffering on cobbles, and want to experience the spring classics' soul rather than chase personal records.

videos.title: Riding the Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo
videos.description:
Bikes, bergs and beers. A few of the great Belgian things. 

The day before Gent-Wevelgem, some 6,700 cyclists tackled the Gent-Wevelgem Cyclo, and VeloNews editor Fred Dreier and Roll Massif's Ben Delaney jumped into the fray. Come along for the ride along the narrow Flemish lanes, the gravel Plugstreets and the steep and cobbled Kemmelberg.

Want to get out there yourself? Check out https://cyclinginflanders.cc/

videos.curation_reason: Excellent first-person race recap covering the sportive with specific details about the course including gravel Plugstreets and the Kemmelberg climb.

videos.title: Gent Wevelgem - BelgiumT1956.79
videos.description:
Climb the steep hills in the northwest of France and West Flanders.
Climb the hills of the freezing cold 2013 Gent-Wevelgem, the 75th edition of this well-known cycling classic, won by Peter Sagan. Starting on the flat roads to the north of Cassel in France, you will soon face a challenging ride over the steep hills in the northwest of France and West Flanders in Belgium. The tough and infamous Kemmelberg has to be climbed twice before ending at the Menen Gate in Ieper. The total distance of the film is 83.5 km.

videos.title: Gent Wevelgem 2008 - Kemmelberg (Peleton 2nd time up)
videos.description: Riders coming up the Kemmelberg the second time. Ballan and Flecha applying some pressure to break the field.

videos.title: Marta Bastianelli Ale Cipollini wins Gent - Wevelgem
videos.description: Former world champion, Marta Bastianelli Ale Cipollini wins the 5th round of the UCI women’s world tour in Belgium

quotes.text: Come along for the ride along the narrow Flemish lanes, the gravel Plugstreets and the steep and cobbled Kemmelberg. The kime berg kicking up to 22% I was happy to have a 3528 low gear with a new SRAM axis and yes if I had a lower gear you better believe I would have used it.

One thing I love about riding in Belgium is the seemingly endless array of quiet narrow winding roads. It feels to this American like the roadie equivalent of single track - the quick hard turns, undulating hills and changing road surfaces keep you in the flow.

key_challenges.name: Kimelberg
key_challenges.description: Steep climb kicking up to 22%. One rider mentioned being happy to have a 35x28 low gear and would have used an even lower gear if available.

key_challenges.name: Plug Streets
key_challenges.description: The area's fairly tame gravel roads described as gravel sections where hikers and cyclists play.

terrain_notes.text: Seemingly endless array of quiet narrow winding roads that feels like the roadie equivalent of single track with quick hard turns, undulating hills and changing road surfaces that keep you in the flow.
Course includes wind, bumpy streets, cobblestones and climbs throughout the route.

gear_mentions.text: For the Kimelberg climb (22% grade), rider was happy to have a 35x28 low gear with new SRAM Axis and mentioned would have used an even lower gear if available.

race_day_tips.text: You roll at Gent-Wevelgem whenever you like, providing flexibility in start times to manage the 6,700 people on the narrow roads.

additional_quotes.text: People here are entirely comfortable riding in close quarters. It is a far cry from your average American charity ride where many folks don't want their wheels anywhere near yours.
There are waffles and chocolate in the feed zones.

rider_intel.search_text: The Gent-Wevelgem sportive features narrow winding roads with quick hard turns, undulating hills, and changing road surfaces including cobblestones and gravel sections. Key challenges include the Kimelberg climb reaching 22% grade where riders recommend low gearing like 35x28, and the Plug Streets gravel roads described as fairly tame. The course includes wind, bumpy streets, and climbs throughout. Riders can start whenever they like to manage crowds of 6,700 people on narrow roads. The area is steeped in World War I history. Feed zones offer Belgian specialties like waffles and chocolate. Local cycling culture embraces close-quarters riding with strong technical skills. The route offers distance options of 140k or 215k, finishing on the same stretch as the professional race.
